Flow (around sphere must be) laminar Or Flow (around sphere is) not turbulent (1) Use of upthrust = weight of displaced fluid U = 5.2 × 10 −7 (N) Example calculation U = 5.3 × 10 −11 m × 998 kg m −3 × 9.81 N kg −1 = 5.19 × 10 −7 N (1) (1) Use of V = πr Use of F = 6πηrv F = 1.5 × 10 −7 N Or Required v = 0.12 m s −1 Or Required V = 2.4 × 10 −9 m Or Required r = 8.3 × 10 −4 m and r = 2.3 × 10 −4 m Or Required η = 3.4 × 10 −3 Pa s Valid conclusion by comparison of relevant student values (ecf from (b)(i)) Example calculation 5.3 × 10 −11 m = πr r = √ 3×5.3 × 10 −11 m 4π = 2.33 × 10 −4 m If Stokes' law applies, F = U F = 6π × 9.5 × 10 −4 Pa s × 2.33 × 10 −4 m × 3.50 × 10 −2 m s −1 = 1.46 × 10 −7 N ≠ 5.19 × 10 −7 N ⸫ Stokes' law does not apply (1) (1) (1) (1) Total for question 16 7 Number Answer Mark Equates horizontal component with force from current F = 480 (N) Example calculation F sin 33° = 260 N F = 260 N ÷ sin 33° = 477 N (1) (1) Resolves vertical component of F Equates vertical forces Weight of buoy = 2500 N (ecf from (a)(i)) Example calculation 477 N cos 33° = 400 N 400 N + Weight of buoy = 2.9 × 10 N Weight of buoy = 2900 N − 400 N = 2500 N (1) (1) (1)

EITHER Horizontal component of F increases (to maintain equilibrium) and Vertical component of F remains the same (because vertical forces do not change, upthrust and weight are constant) F = F h + F v so F increases [dependent on MP1] tan θ = F h /F v so θ increases [dependent on MP1] OR Horizontal component of F increases (to maintain equilibrium) and Vertical component of F remains the same (because vertical forces do not change, upthrust and weight are constant) F = F h + F v so F increases [dependent on MP1] Or tan θ = F h /F v so θ increases [dependent on MP1] F cos θ is constant so increase in either F or θ implies an increase in the other [dependent on MP2] (1) (1) (1) (1) (1) (1) Total for question 17 8 Number Answer Mark *18 This question assesses a student’s ability to show a coherent and logically structured answer with linkages and fully-sustained reasoning. Marks are awarded for indicative content and for how the answer is structured and shows lines of reasoning. The following table shows how the marks should be awarded for indicative content and lines of reasoning. Use of ΔW = F Δx or ΔE grav = mgΔh Use of P = W / t Use of efficiency = useful power output / total power input Or Use of efficiency = useful energy output / total energy input Total power input = 2.0 × 10 W Example of calculation ΔW = 4.4 × 10 × 15 m = 6.6 × 10 J Output power = 6.6 × 10 J ÷ 70 s = 9.4 × 10 W Efficiency = 9.4×10 W Total power input =0.47 Total power input = 9.4 × 10 푊 0.47 = 2.0 × 10 W (1) (1) (1) (1) Total clockwise must balance total anticlockwise moment (about tower) Or Net/resultant/total moment (about tower) must be zero. Total anticlockwise moment is due to counterweight and Total clockwise moment is due to sum of moment from beam and load Increasing the distance of the load increases the (cw) moment (distance of CoG remains the same) Distance of counterweight needs to be increased [dependent on MP3] (1) (1) (1) (1) Use of moment = F x Position of centre of mass of beam identified Use of principle of moments Maximum distance of load = 15 m Or, for load at 22 m ... CW moment =1.18×10 Nm and max ASW moment = 8.8 ×10 Nm Or Required distance of counterweight = 11 m Or Required counterweight = 1.5 ×10 N Or Maximum load = 3.0 ×10 N Or Required distance to CoG = 2.9 m Or Required weight of beam = 1.3 ×10 N Or Resultant moment = 3.0 × 10 Nm (cw) Valid conclusion by comparison of relevant student values Example of calculation For equilibrium: 3.0 ×10 N × 7.0 m + 4.4 ×10 N × x = 1.1×10 N × 8.0 m where x is the maximum distance of the load from the tower. x = (8.8×10 Nm − 2.1 × 10 Nm) ÷ 4.4 ×10 N = 6.7 × 10 Nm ÷ 4.4 ×10 N = 15.2 m 15.2 m < 22.0 m so crane would topple if load moved to end of beam (1) (1) (1) (1) (1) Total for question 19 13 Number Answer Mark

Stress (or strain) value at/beyond which a material/object undergoes a sudden or large plastic deformation (1) The force/tension from/in the cable (on the actor) is greater than the weight of the actor (So) there is a resultant/net/unbalanced force (upwards) (1) (1) Use of W = m g Use of ΣF = m a Tension = 917 (N) Example calculation W= 77 kg × 9.81 N kg −1 = 755 N T – 755 N = 77 kg × 2.1 m s −2 T = 162 N + 755 N = 917 N (1) (1) (1) Use of A = π r Use of σ = F ÷ A (ecf from (b)(ii)) Allowed stress (15% yield stress of steel) = 3.8 × 10 Pa and σ = 2.0 × 10 Pa Or (15%) −1 of cable stress = 1.3 × 10 Pa Or Max safe tension = 1.7 × 10 N Or Min safe diameter = 5.6 × 10 −3 m Or Percentage of yield stress = 8% Or Max safe acceleration = 12.3 m s −2 Valid conclusion by comparison of relevant student values Example calculation A = 휋푑 = 휋× ( 7.6×10 −3 m ) =4.54×10 −5 m 휎= 917 N 4.54×10 −5 m =2.02×10 Pa (show that value gives 2.03 × 10 Pa) 0.15×2.5×10 Pa=3.75×10 Pa 2.03×10 Pa < 3.75×10 Pa so it is safe. (1) (1) (1) (1)

New cable has a greater cross sectional area, (but same breaking stress) so a greater force is required Because new cable has smaller Young modulus, there is a greater strain for the same stress So (at breaking stress) there will be a greater extension (because cables are the same length) [dependent on MP2] Or Smaller Young modulus implies greater extension (at breaking stress, because cables are the same length) [independent mark] (And as) force and extension both increase, work done to break the new cable is greater than that for the original cable [independent mark] (1) (1) (1) (1) Total for question 20 14 Pearson Education Limited.
